title: Allocating Global Equities in a Taxable Account | Dimensional
gist: Dimensional research argues that, contrary to the conventional advice to shelter non-US equities in tax-advantaged accounts, investors in higher tax brackets can lower their tax cost by OVERWEIGHTING US equities inside taxable accounts. The driver is that US equities carry lower dividend yields and higher qualified-dividend percentages than international and emerging markets, and the foreign tax credit only partially offsets the gap; with Dimensional funds distributing near-zero capital gains, dividend taxation becomes the dominant cross-regional tax cost.

insights.claim: Investors in higher tax brackets may lower their total tax cost by overweighting US equities within taxable accounts, reversing the conventional guidance to overweight US equities in tax-advantaged accounts instead.
insights.speaker: Kaitlin Hendrix, CFA, Asset Allocation Research Director and Vice President
insights.so_what: Actionable asset-location switch for high-bracket taxable investors: tilt the taxable sleeve toward US equities, push international/EM into sheltered accounts.
insights.quote: Yet our research shows that investors in higher tax brackets may also be able to lower their tax costs by overweighting US equities within taxable accounts.

insights.claim: For each 10% shift of a portfolio into US equities away from developed ex-US or emerging markets equities, Dimensional estimates the overall portfolio's tax cost falls by roughly 3 to 5 basis points.
insights.speaker: Kaitlin Hendrix, CFA, Asset Allocation Research Director and Vice President
insights.so_what: Quantifies the payoff of the tilt so an advisor can size the US overweight against tracking-error/diversification trade-offs.
insights.quote: For each 10% increase to US equities away from developed ex US or emerging markets equities, the tax cost of the overall portfolio is likely to decrease by 3–5 basis points.

insights.claim: Over 2025 the Dimensional US Core Equity 2 ETF (DFAC) had a dividend yield of 0.98%, while the International (DFIC) and Emerging Markets (DFEM) Core Equity 2 ETFs yielded 2.54% and 2.33% respectively — more than double the US fund.
insights.speaker: Kaitlin Hendrix, CFA, Asset Allocation Research Director and Vice President
insights.so_what: Higher non-US dividend yields create a larger taxable income stream, so location of international equities materially affects after-tax return.
insights.quote: the dividend income as a percentage of average NAV (net asset value), or dividend yield, for the Dimensional US Core Equity 2 ETF (DFAC) was 0.98%. The Dimensional International Core Equity 2 ETF (DFIC) and Dimensional Emerging Markets Core Equity 2 ETF (DFEM), by comparison, had a dividend yield more than twice that: 2.54% and 2.33%, respectively.

insights.claim: In 2025 the qualified dividend income share was 100% for DFAC (US), 96% for DFIC (international), and only 50% for DFEM (emerging markets), meaning a large fraction of EM dividends is taxed at ordinary-income rates rather than the lower long-term capital gains rate.
insights.speaker: Kaitlin Hendrix, CFA, Asset Allocation Research Director and Vice President
insights.so_what: EM dividends are doubly disadvantaged (higher yield, low QDI), making EM the least tax-efficient regional sleeve to hold in a taxable account.
insights.quote: QDI for the Dimensional US Core Equity 2 ETF in 2025 was 100%, versus 96% for the Dimensional International Core Equity 2 ETF and 50% for the Dimensional Emerging Markets Core Equity 2 ETF.

insights.claim: Qualified dividend income percentages are typically lower outside the US, partly because of the absence of tax treaties with some markets.
insights.speaker: Kaitlin Hendrix, CFA, Asset Allocation Research Director and Vice President
insights.so_what: Explains the structural reason non-US dividends are taxed harder, which won't reverse without treaty changes — a durable tilt rationale.
insights.quote: Qualified dividend income percentages are typically lower outside the US due to the lack of tax treaties with some markets, among other reasons.

insights.claim: The foreign tax credit on non-US dividends can only be claimed when the related income is subject to US taxation, so it is unavailable for non-US stocks held in tax-deferred accounts like a 401k or IRA, where foreign withholding tax reduces returns with no offsetting credit.
insights.speaker: Kaitlin Hendrix, CFA, Asset Allocation Research Director and Vice President
insights.so_what: Reframes the classic 'shelter international in the IRA' advice: doing so forfeits the FTC, a hidden cost of sheltering foreign equities.
insights.quote: Income earned in tax-deferred accounts, such as a 401k or an IRA, is not subject to US taxation, and therefore the investor cannot claim the FTC for income earned in such accounts.
insights.locator: Foreign Dividend Withholding Tax and Foreign Tax Credit section

insights.claim: Even with the foreign tax credit, the FTC does not fully offset the higher tax cost of non-US equities: in 2025 the FTC for DFIC was 0.21% of NAV but the DFAC-vs-DFIC tax-cost gap was still 0.32%, and for DFEM the FTC was 0.35% with a residual 0.49% gap versus DFAC.
insights.speaker: Kaitlin Hendrix, CFA, Asset Allocation Research Director and Vice President
insights.so_what: Net of the FTC, US equities remain materially cheaper to hold in a taxable account, supporting the US overweight conclusion.
insights.quote: In 2025, the FTC for DFIC was 0.21% as a percentage of NAV, but still the difference in tax cost between DFAC and DFIC was 0.32%. For DFEM, the FTC was 0.35%, still resulting in a 0.49% difference in tax cost versus DFAC.
insights.locator: Foreign Dividend Withholding Tax and Foreign Tax Credit section

insights.claim: None of Dimensional's equity ETFs and the majority of its equity mutual funds distributed zero short- and long-term capital gains in 2025, shifting the cross-regional tax differential almost entirely onto dividend income.
insights.speaker: Kaitlin Hendrix, CFA, Asset Allocation Research Director and Vice President
insights.so_what: With capital-gains distributions neutralized, dividend taxation is the lever that matters — which is exactly where the US-vs-international gap lives.
insights.quote: none of Dimensional's equity ETFs distributed capital gains in 2025. And while mutual funds are often associated with greater capital gains distributions than ETFs, the majority of Dimensional's equity mutual funds distributed zero short- and long-term capital gains in 2025.

insights.claim: In calendar year 2025 Dimensional's mutual funds traded $27.6 billion in kind and transferred out $19.5 billion in net gains, using in-kind redemptions to reduce security weights without realizing taxable gains.
insights.speaker: Kaitlin Hendrix, CFA, Asset Allocation Research Director and Vice President
insights.so_what: Demonstrates a concrete mechanism (in-kind redemptions) by which Dimensional's mutual funds achieve ETF-like tax efficiency; relevant when comparing vehicle structures.
insights.quote: In calendar year 2025, Dimensional's mutual funds traded $27.6 billion in kind and transferred out $19.5 billion in net gains.

insights.claim: Because US REITs must distribute at least 90% of taxable income annually and that income is generally nonqualified, REITs carry high, ordinary-rate-taxed dividends, so investors may prefer to hold REIT exposure in tax-advantaged accounts; most Dimensional equity funds and its Tax-Sensitive Wealth Models exclude REITs.
insights.speaker: Kaitlin Hendrix, CFA, Asset Allocation Research Director and Vice President
insights.so_what: REITs are a prime candidate for the tax-advantaged sleeve; standalone REIT funds let investors locate that exposure deliberately.
insights.quote: US REITs are required to distribute at least 90% of taxable income to shareholders annually in the form of dividends. As a result, dividend yields are generally higher for REITs than other equity securities.

source_meta.author: Kaitlin Hendrix, CFA, Asset Allocation Research Director and Vice President
